heading: Step 2: Name, describe, and define your Atlas Resource Policy.
body:
- Give your Atlas Resource Policy a unique name.

- (Optional) Add a description.

- Copy and modify the following example policies for your organization,
  then paste the code in the **Cedar Policy** field.

  - [Restrict Number of Clusters](restrict-number-of-clusters-ui)

  - [Restrict Cloud Provider](restrict-cloud-provider-ui)

  - [Restrict Cloud Provider Region](restrict-region-ui)

  - [Restrict IP Addresses](restrict-ip-addresses-ui)

  - [Restrict cluster Tier Sizes](restrict-cluster-tier-ui)

  - [Require Project Maintenance Windows](require-maintenance-window-ui)

  - [Prevent Modifications to Network Peering](prevent-peering-modifications-ui)

  - [Prevent Modifications to Private Endpoints](prevent-private-endpoint-modifications-ui)

  - [Restrict TLS (Transport Layer Security) Protocol and Cipher Suites](restrict-tls-ui)

  - [Enforce Disk Size (GB)](enforce-disk-size-ui)

  - [Enforce Cluster Topology (Replica Set, Sharded, or Global Cluster)](restrict-cluster-type-ui)

  - [Enforce Minimum Shard Count](restrict-shard-count-ui)

  - [Enforce Dedicated Config Servers for Sharded Clusters](enforce-config-server-management-mode-ui)

  > **Note:** We recommend creating multiple, simple Atlas Resource Policies to make tracking easier.
  > For example, if you want to restrict cloud provider and multiple regions,
  > consider creating one Atlas Resource Policy that restricts the cloud provider and another
  > Atlas Resource Policy that restricts the regions.
